coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of polyamine-functionalized carbon dots from xylan and their use for the detection of tannic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Pei
Zhu, Ziqi
Chen, Minzhi
Zhou, Xiaoyan
Chen, Weimin
coredata.dc:description: A facile and straightforward microwave-assisted method was used to prepare polyamine-functionalized carbon dots (CDs) from a precursor comprising renewable xylan and branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI). The as-prepared BPEI-CDs were monodispersed sphere particles with an average diameter of about 8.62 nm, and exhibited excellent fluorescent property and high stability, as well as excitation-independent emission behavior. Furthermore, it is attractive that the BPEI-CDs can be used as novel fluorescent probes for detecting tannic acid (TA) sensitively and selectively. At the optimum condition, the TA detection system was established in water solution and ethanol solution with a dynamic range from 0.1 to 5 μM, and their detection limit of 36.8 nM and 44.9 nM were also determined, respectively. Most importantly, the BPEI-CDs-based sensors can be successfully applied to detect TA in real lake water and white wine samples, suggesting the low-cost and excellent BPEI-CDs are potential suitable for TA detection in practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Xylan
Polyethyleneimine
Carbon quantum dots
Tannic acid
Detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TA and Interference Experiments
Morphology and Chemical Structure of the BPEI-CDs
Sensitivity and Selectivity of TA Detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the BPEI-CDs, inserts is the particles size distribution. (b) XRD pattern of the BPEI-CDs. (c) FTIR spectrum of the BPEI-CDs. (d) XPS survey spectrum. (e) C1s spectrum, and (f) N1s spectrum of BPEI-CQDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum and maximum PL emission spectrum of the BPEI-CQDs. Inset: photograph of BPEI-CDs aqueous solution taken under visible and UV light. (b) PL emission spectra of the BPEI-CQDs with different excitation wavelengths. (c) The color coordinates of the BPEI-CDs. (d) Fluorescence decay curve of the BPEI-CDs irradiated at 410 nm and the fitting equation is shown in the corresponding panel.
(a) Effect of pH on the PL intensities of the BPEI-CQDs. (b) Effect of pH on the quenching efficiency of BPEI-CQDs in the presence of TA.

(a) The maximum PL emission spectra of BPEI-CQDs in water solution upon addition of various concentration of TA, inset shows the exponential relationship between the F 0/F and TA concentration (0–100 μM). The linear relationship between F 0/F and TA concentration (0.1–5 μM) in water solution (b) and ethanol solution (c). (d) The PL intensity of aqueous BPEI-CDs solution (0.25 mg/mL) in the presence of interfering substances.
A schematic illustration of the synthesis of BPEI-CDs and the method of TA detection.
The comparison of this fluorescent method with previous works.

row.entry: Single-wall carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) modified glassy carbon electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of TA in real lake water samples and white wine samples.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of polyamine-functionalized carbon dots from xylan and their use for the detection of tannic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and straightforward microwave-assisted method was used to prepare polyamine-functionalized carbon dots (CDs) from a precursor comprising renewable xylan and branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI). The as-prepared BPEI-CDs were monodispersed sphere particles with an average diameter of about 8.62 nm, and exhibited excellent fluorescent property and high stability, as well as excitation-independent emission behavior. Furthermore, it is attractive that the BPEI-CDs can be used as novel fluorescent probes for detecting tannic acid (TA) sensitively and selectively. At the optimum condition, the TA detection system was established in water solution and ethanol solution with a dynamic range from 0.1 to 5 μM, and their detection limit of 36.8 nM and 44.9 nM were also determined, respectively. Most importantly, the BPEI-CDs-based sensors can be successfully applied to detect TA in real lake water and white wine samples, suggesting the low-cost and excellent BPEI-CDs are potential suitable for TA detection in practical application.

ce:section.ce:para: Tannic acid (TA), as a kind of natural phenolic compound exists in a variety of plants, exhibits many advantages, such as antimutagenic, antioxidant, anticarcinogenic and antitoxic activities [1]. Due to these special physiological and pharmacological activities, TA is an important component of medicine for therapy of burns, diarrhea and poison. Besides its pharmaceutical effect, TA is also used as clarifying agent in the brewing and wine industry, while the content of this food additive is also an essential standard to evaluate their quality. Most importantly, TA is one of the main persistent organic pollutants in leather industrial sewage and can cause serious problems for the environment, this pollutant is harmful to the root system of plants through toxic tannic acid-metal complex that formed easily from the interaction between TA and metal in most surface and ground water. Therefore, the determination of TA is very important for food analysis, medical studies as well as sewage detection. To date, various methods have been used for the TA determination, including spectrophotometry [2], fluorescence [3], electrochemistry [4], liquid chromatography [5], thin-layer chromatography [6], and protein precipitation [7]. However, the most of current approaches have suffered from certain disadvantages, such as poor specificity and selectivity, instability of reagents, expensive equipment or standardization difficulties. Thus, developing highly sensitive, simple and cost-efficient techniques for the estimation of TA level is of great significance.

Currently, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), a relatively new member of carbon family, have attracted much attention due to their charming advantages, such as excellent photoluminescence (PL), high stability, low toxicity and environmental friendliness relative to semiconductor quantum dots and traditional organic dyes. Apart from the use of CDs for biological labeling [8], photocatalysis [9] and optoelectronic devices [10], CDs have been demonstrated to be suitable for sensing various heavy metal ions, including Cu2+ [11,12], Hg2+ [13–16], Fe3+ [17,18], Cr6+ [19,20] and Au3+ [21]. Since the CDs-based sensors with high sensitivity, rapid analysis, simple operation, and excellent interference resistance ability, The functionalized CDs were also confirmed to be promising sensing platforms for detecting several molecules, such as glucose [22], dopamine [23,24], picric acid [25,26] and ascorbic acid [20,27], Thus, the functionalized CDs that highly dependent on the selection of precursors and passivant are also feasible for TA detection. To the best of our knowledge, only a handful reports have synthesized the functionalized CDs for TA detection, for instance, Ahmed et al. prepared CDs-based sensors for detecting TA by using 6-bromohexylboronic acid as carbon source [28], but certain critical problems still exist, including high cost of the starting materials, very low quantum yields (QYs) and poor PL intensity. Liu et al. based on the enhancement effect of chemiluminescence to determine TA. However, these CDs-based nanosensors have suffered from time consuming synthesis route and the involvement of complicated test procedure [29]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop simple and efficient CDs-based sensors for TA detection.

Although many efforts has been made to synthesize CDs in last decades, the efficient one-step synthesis following bottom-up route for the rapid production of CDs from biomass-based precursors is still challengeable. Herein, the low-cost and renewable xylan, which is rich in hemicellulose, was used as a potential carbon source for CDs production in hydrothermal condition. For further improving the fluorescent efficiency of the CDs and providing active sites on the surface of CDs to recognize analytes, the branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI) with polyamine structure was chosen as the functionalizing and capping regent to passivate the xylan-derived CDs. Considering microwave irradiation which allows for intensive, efficient and homogeneous energy distribution is more effective than the traditional hydrothermal treatment in accelerating the reaction rate in an aqueous phase, a microwave reactor that features temperature control system was used to synthesize BPEI functionalized CDs (BPEI-CDs). Due to the strong interaction of reactants with microwave irradiation and the fast heating rate can be achieved, the synthetic process can be finished in one-step within just a few minutes. These as-prepared fluorescent nanoparticles were found to be sensitive and selective probes for detecting TA (Scheme 1 ). In particular, the established detecting platform for TA can be directly used in real lake water and white wine samples. Moreover, the possible mechanism for the newly established detection system was also discussed. Such reliable sensors along with the efficient synthesis strategy will afford a new way for TA quantitative determination as well as improve the add-value of biomass-based CDs.

Xylan (85%), BPEI, quinine sulfate, TA, gallic acid, sucrose, tartaric acid, oxalic acid and citric acid were all purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sulfate acid, sodium hydroxide and ethyl alcohol were procured from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) with different pH values were obtained from Haibiao Technologies Co., Ltd. (Fuzhou, China). All other reagents were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Xylan was purified using sodium hydroxide and ethyl alcohol [30,31]. All other reagents were used as received. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment.

The microwave synthesis system was commercially available and produced by Chemistry Electronic Microwave Company, (DISCOVER SP, USA). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were captured by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100 UHR microscope, JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a Bruker AXS D8 advanced powder X-ray diffraction system with a Cu Kα radiation source (Bruker Co., Germany). Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was carried out on a Vertex 80 V FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on an AXIS UltraDLD spectrometer (Shimadzu, UK) using Mg as the exciting source. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a Lambda 950 UV–Visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). For PL characterization, all fluorescence spectra of BPEI-CDs were obtained in a quartz cell with 10 mm path length using an F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), the band pass for excitation and emission was set as 10 nm. The surface charge of the BPEI-CDs was measured using a ZEN3600 Zeta Plus (Malvern, UK) instrument with a He-Ne laser source. Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves were measured under excitation at 410 nm using a FLS 980 time-correlated single-photon counting system (EI, UK).

The BPEI-CDs were synthesized by a simple green route of microwave assisted pyrolysis method. Typically, xylan (0.5 g) and BPEI (0.8 g) were dissolved in ultrapure water (10 mL) to form a transparent solution. The mixture was then transferred into reaction tube and heated in the microwave reactor at 180 °C for 15 min. After the reaction was complete and naturally cooled down to room temperature, a resulting dark brown solution was obtained, indicating the formation of BPEI-CDs [32,33]. Subsequently, the suspension of BPEI-CDs was obtained after separating the large particles by centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Prior to further characterization, the solid BPEI-CDs were obtained under vacuum freeze-drying condition.

The quantum yield of the prepared BPEI-CDs was measured by a well-established comparative method using quinine sulfate as standard. In brief, quinine sulfate with QY = 0.54 was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (refractive index of 1.33), while BPEI-CDs were dissolved in ultrapure water (refractive index of 1.33), their absorption values and fluorescent spectra were measured and the QY of BPEI-CDs was calculated by the following equation:
(1)
Q x
=
Q st
Grad x
Grad st
η x 2
η st 2
Here, Q stands for quantum yield. Grad refers to the gradient of the plot of integrated fluorescence peak area against absorbance. η represents the refractive index of the solvent. The subscripts x and st correspond to the BPEI-CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. In particular, the absorbance of BPEI-CDs and quinine sulfate solution in 10-mm quartz cell was adjusted to less than 0.05 to prevent re-absorption effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TA and Interference Experiments
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical assay, 20 μL of synthesized BPEI-CDs suspension was mixed with 5 mL ultrapure water in 10 mL volumetric flask and diluted to volume with 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) at pH 11, which made the concentration of BPEI-CDs was 0.25 mg/mL. For the quantitative determination of TA, the different concentrations of TA were prepared in the above BPEI-CDs solution as working samples after gentle shaking. Subsequently, the maximum PL intensities of all working samples at 340 nm excitation were recorded at room temperature after 20 min reactions. The PL intensity measurements were carried out in triplicate and the average values were recorded. In addition, the linear detection range of TA in the presence of BPEI-CDs was also evaluated in 10% ethanol solution in the same way.

To evaluate the selectivity of the BPEI-CDs-based sensors for TA detection, several representative metal ions, including Fe3+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+ and Cu2+ were dissolved in PBS (0.10 M, pH = 5.0) with a final concentration of 50 μM. In addition, several biological agents, such as oxalic acid, ascorbic acid, tartaric acid, gallic acid, citric acid, glucose and sucrose were also dissolved in PBS (0.10 M, pH = 11) with a final concentration of 30 μM. Then, 0.25 mg/mL BPEI-CDs was added into each prepared stock solution, and the experimental results were obtained in a similar way with TA detection.

The performance of the present method for real water sample analysis was validated by using lake water samples which obtained from the Xuanwu Lake, Nanjing, Jiangsu province, China. The lake water samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and then filtered by 0.22 μm syringe filter. The white wine samples from local supermarket were also used in the application experiment, the white wine samples were diluted until the alcoholic content decreased to 10% and without any further treatment. The as-prepared samples were spiked with different concentration of TA and the detection results were validated by the established calibration curves.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and Chemical Structure of the BPEI-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size of the BPEI-CDs were characterized by HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 1a, the synthesized nanostructures are almost spherical and well separated from each other. Moreover, from the HRTEM image, it can be seen that the single nanoparticle exhibits core–shell structure containing a small carbon core and a coating layer, such composite structure could be highly relate to the BPEI passivation. As judge from about 140 individual particles, the size of BPEI-CDs is mainly distributed in the range of 5–13 nm (inset of Fig. 1a), with an average diameter of about 8.62 nm. Although the detailed mechanism for the formation of carbon Nano dots is obscure, it could be deduced that xylan in thermal aqueous first suffered from dehydration and decomposition and then produced soluble compounds, these soluble compounds experienced polymerization and condensation, and then the carbon cores were finally formed by aromatization and carbonization. The XRD pattern of the as-prepared BPEI-CDs is depicted in Fig. 1b. The pattern exhibits a broad peak at about 20°, revealing amorphous carbon is contained in the BPEI-CDs [34].

The FTIR spectrum was recorded to witness structural insights about the BPEI-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1c, the intense band of 3430 cm−1 is assigned to the OH stretching vibrations, the characteristic absorption peaks of CH emerge at 2925 cm−1 and 2834 cm−1, the sharp peak at 1629 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of CC that reveals the skeleton structure of BPEI-CDs. Moreover, the band of 1346 cm−1 is corresponding to the bending vibration of COH, while the peaks at 1115 cm−1 is due to the vibration of COC and 1043 cm−1 represents CO stretching vibration, which predicts multiple oxygen-containing groups on the surface of BPEI-CDs. Furthermore, the dull band of 3250 cm−1 and the obvious band of 1460 cm−1 are assigned to the NH and CN stretching vibration, indicating the formation of amino groups and amide groups on BPEI-CDs surfaces during the synthesis process. Towards the purpose of further gaining insight into the chemical structure information of the current BPEI-CDs, XPS survey spectra were performed. As shown in Fig. 1d, the full-range spectrum exhibits four bonding energy peaks at 285.1, 399.2, 496.0 and 531.1 eV, which correspond to C1s, N1s, Na1s, and O1s, respectively. Specifically, the existence of Na is stem from the residues of NaOH in the refinement process of xylan. The high-resolution C1s scans (Fig. 1e) show four different chemical bonds, corresponding to the CC/CC (284.4 eV), CN (285.1 eV), CO (285.7 eV) and CO (287.5 eV), respectively [13]. Specifically, the CC/CC that formed during intramolecular dehydration was regarded as the main components of skeleton of CDs, the existence of CO components is related to baring hydroxyl or COC, indicting the intermolecular dehydration occurred in the formation of BPEI-CDs. Besides, peak area of CO is very small in Fig. 1e, which suggests a small amount of carboxyl groups in the BPEI-CDs. the large peak area of CN demonstrates that BPEI participated in the synthesis of BPEI-CDs and high proportion of nitrogen elements were doped in these nanostructures, which is also witnessed by Fig. 1f. The N1s spectrum exhibits two peaks at 398.7 and 400.5 eV, which are related to the bonding energies of the -NH2 and CN, respectively [35]. In addition, zeta potential result demonstrates that the synthesized BPEI-CDs are positively charged (2.59 mV), thus further confirming the attachment of surface-contained N-related groups. All the results from FTIR spectrum and XPS analysis confirm that the prepared BPEI-CDs are rich in hydrophilic functional groups which endow them to be more suitable for fluorescent detection, photocatalyst, cell imaging, and so on.

The optical performance of BPEI-CDs was investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 2a, the resultant BPEI-CDs show a typical absorption peak at 335 nm. This absorption behavior, which leads to the strong fluorescence, can be related to the trapping of excited state energy of the surface functional groups [25]. The maximum fluorescence emission of BPEI-CDs centered at 463 nm with 340 nm excitation can be observed, suggesting a bright blue emission performance of the synthesized BPEI-CDs. Moreover, the emission spectra were recorded for further confirming the optical property. As shown in Fig. 2b, a typical excitation-independent PL emission spectra with a peak at 463 nm is observed for BPEI-CDs as the excitation wavelength increase from 340 to 400 nm. Due to the particles vary in size, while the emissive surface trap sites of the BPEI-CDs should be rather uniform owing to the BPEI modification. Thus, the uniform surface state rather than quantum confinement effect is responsible for the excitation-independent fluorescent property [36,37]. In addition, the chromaticity coordinates were (x = 0.147, y = 0.168) in blue light region when the emission caused by the irradiation with 365 nm UV light (Fig. 2c), which further confirm a typical blue emission behavior of BPEI-CDs. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of BPEI-CDs was measured to further investigate the origin of fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 2d, the fluorescence decay curves can be fitted by a double-exponential formula, revealing the lifetimes of components τ1 and τ2. The average lifetime of 5.80 ns is determined for BPEI-CDs, and the components τ1 share as high as 99.9%, which indicates the low density of defect states and the radiative electron transition is mostly responsible for the PL emission of the BPEI-CDs [38,39]. Meanwhile, the quantum yield of the BPEI-CDs is measured to be 8.0% by using quinine sulfate as a reference (Fig. S1). Most importantly, the synthesized BPEI-CDs have no photobleaching after irradiation with UV lamp at 365 nm for 300 min (Fig. S2), and without any aggregation even after three months in air. In addition, the PL intensity of BPEI-CDs hardly exhibit variations along with the increasing concentration of NaCl (Fig. S3). Besides their high stability, the high PL intensity and excitation-independent performance of the prepared BPEI-CDs which can avoid autofluorescence are more favorable for the application in quantitative assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and Selectivity of TA Detection
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the significant effect of TA on environment and human health, it is obviously important to develop efficient TA detection method which can monitor TA level sensitively and selectively in aqueous system. It was found that the synthesized BPEI-CDs meet well with the requirements of a fluorescent probe. Most importantly, the establishment of optimum condition for TA detection by using the BPEI-CDs is necessary for accurate determination. Several studies have confirmed that the PL intensity is susceptible to acid-base environments, while the as-prepared BPEI-CDs are insensitive to the pH values in the range of 3.0–11.0 (Fig. 3a). This performance could be mainly related to the polyethyleneimine passivation. Specifically, numerous previous researches regarded that the electron density of bare CDs could be changed due to the protonation or deprotonation of carboxyl groups on the surface of bare CDs, while the BPEI-CDs that functionalized by polyethyleneimine keep strong linkage (-CONH-) between the –COOH groups of carbonized xylan and -NH2 groups of BPEI. Thus the BPEI performed as a protection layer after the formation of nanoparticles, which is beneficial for the fluorescence stability of BPEI-CDs in both acidity and basicity environment. However, the PL intensities are various when the BPEI-CDs were mixed with TA (30 μM) at different pH values. As shown in Fig. 3b, the effect of pH on the sensing performance of BPEI-CDs to TA is recorded by monitoring the dependence of the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F 0/F), where F 0 and F represent the PL intensities of BPEI-CDs in the absence and presence of TA, respectively. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (F 0/F) shows an almost increase tendency with the pH values increase from 3 to 11, while the stability of BPEI-CDs in the same range of pH values has been demonstrated. Thus, one hypothesis for this performance is that the influence of TA on the BPEI-CDs can be enhanced in alkaline environments. Indeed, TA is a weak organic acid which ionization is highly related to the variation of pH, it has been demonstrated that the TA at lower pH is close to the point of zero charge and presents in molecular form, while the increasing of pH is more likely to result in a severe increase in the negative charge and dissociation degree of TA [40]. It can be assumed that the dissociated TA unit with approximately four negative charges has capacity to facilitate the electron transfer process from the photo excited BPEI-CDs to the aromatic groups in TA, thus enhancing the non-radiative transition of BPEI-CDs and inducing a high fluorescence quenching efficiency. Based on the above-mentioned analysis, the PBS buffer with a pH value of 11 was selected as the solvent in the following experiment for a best detection results.

For further investigate the sensitivity of the fluorescence assay for quantitative detection of TA, the different concentration of TA was added to the aqueous solution of BPEI-CDs (0.25 mg/mL), and the corresponding emission spectra and intensities were recorded at 340 nm excitation at the optimum conditions. As shown in Fig. 4a, TA has no influence on the peak position of the maximum emission spectra even its concentration reached to 30 μM, while the PL intensity decreased gradually with the increasing concentration of TA in the range of 0–100 μM (insets in the Fig. 4a). Further, the concentration-dependent curve can be plotted following an exponential quenching equation: F 0/F = A exp. (k[α]) + B, where A, B and k are constants, and the obtained equation was: F 0/F = 23.45 exp. (0.04[α]) − 30.57 (R2 = 0.996), this well fitted model for the analytes detection in the whole concentration range is in agreement with what Li et al. outlined [41]. As TA units at pH 11 can act as electron transition sites, the increasing concentration of TA which, as a consequence, increases the non-radiative transition of BPEI-CDs. Additionally, the BPEI-CDs can be wrapped easily by the increased dendritic TA, which is another possible reason for the enhancement of fluorescence quenching efficiency. Fig. 4b presents a nice linear relationship between the quenching efficiency (F 0 /F) versus TA concentration (C TA) in the range of 0.1 to 5 μM, the fitted curve equation is F 0 /F = 0.167 C TA + 0.973, and the corresponding correlation coefficient is 0.997, suggesting accuracy and reliability of the fluorescence assay for TA detection. The lowest detection limit (LOD) at a signal-to-noise of 3 is about 36.8 nM. Apart from an excellent detection result can be obtained in aqueous solution, the BPEI-CDs exhibit nice performance in ethanol solution as well. As depicted in Fig. 4c, the regression equations for TA determination is F 0 /F = 0.137 C TA + 1.001 (R 2 = 0.998), and the LOD is 44.9 nM.

The selectivity is another essential property for fluorescent probes. Thus, the PL intensities of BPEI-CDs were measured with and without the potentially interfering substances. As illustrate in Fig. 4c, the representative metal ions and biological agents, including Fe3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, TA, oxalic acid, ascorbic acid, tartaric acid, gallic acid, citric acid, glucose and sucrose, can hardly induce fluorescence quenching except TA, indicating the BPEI-CDs-based sensors exhibit high selectivity for detecting TA. According to previous reports, the fluorescence quenching induced by metal ions can be attributed to the chelate complex that formed from the combination of metal ions with the amino groups or carboxyl groups [17,42–45]. However, the resulting BPEI-CDs has demonstrated their almost pH-independent PL behavior, which indicates that the amino and carboxyl groups could not be related to the PL centers of BPEI-CDs, thus the surface-chelating metal ions have almost no influence on the PL intensity. Based on the quenching mechanism of photoinduced electron transfer [41,46,47], the state levels of interfering molecules could not be matched with the energy gap of BPEI-CDs, and hence prevent the formation of complex that can delocalize the photo-induced electrons in BPEI-CDs, while the interaction between BPEI-CDs and TA is more likely to promote the excited electron transfer from the BPEI-CDs to TA and finally cause the fluorescence quenching. To prove the fluorescence quenching is related to the non-radiative transition, the effect of TA on the fluorescence lifetime and Zeta potential of BPEI-CDs were investigate carefully. The corresponding results demonstrate that the lifetime increased from 5.80 ns to 6.03 ns and the Zeta potential increased from 2.59 mV to 9.25 mV after adding 30 μM of TA, the slight change of fluorescence lifetime and significant increase in Zeta potential reveal that the TA detection is dependent on the non-radiative transition induced fluorescence quenching. Compared with previous reports (see Table 1 ), the proposed assay method has great application prospect in terms of the low cost and rapid of probe, as well as its high sensitivity and selectivity.

To evaluate the BPEI-CDs-based sensors for TA detection in practical application, the established sensing platform in water solution and ethanol solution were applied to detect TA in the lake water and white wine, respectively. The test results are listed in Table 2 . The recovery of TA by the standard addition has been obtained to evaluate the veracity of the proposed assay method. It can be seen that the spiked recovery values varied from 95.2 to 112.0% for lake water samples and 94.0 to 109.9% for white wine samples, suggesting the BPEI-CDs-based sensors are capable of detecting TA in practical application.

In summary, polyamine-functionalized CDs were synthesized by simple one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis xylan in the presence of BPEI as a passivant. Besides the usage of low cost and sustainable carbon source in the synthesis method, the BPEI-CDs can be obtained at a low temperature within just 15 min. These nanoparticles with excitation-independent PL properties were found to be promising sensors for detecting TA in aqueous solution and ethanol solution. Ultimately, the developed detection system was validated by using the BPEI-CDs to detect TA in real lake water and white wine samples. This work will open a simple and efficient pathway to the detection of TA in practical application.

This work was supported by the Jiangsu Nature Science Foundation (BK20161524), the Program for 333 Talents Project in Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BRA2016381), the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), and the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX17_0839), the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD) and the Doctorate Fellowship Foundation of Nanjing Forestry University (2017).
None.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.01.043.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of tannic acid based on luminol chemiluminescence catalyzed by cupric oxide nanoparticles
